title: First recorded Viking raid - Isle of Portland, Dorset 789
content: This is the earliest documented Viking encounter in England per the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle: three ships (likely Norwegian) landed, and a royal official (Beaduheard) was killed after a misunderstanding. It predates the more famous 793 raid.
attribution: By Wilson44691 at English Wikipedia - Photograph taken by Mark A. Wilson (Department of Geology, The College of Wooster).[1], Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=3796792

title: Raid on Lindisfarne monastery - Holy Island, Northumberland 793
content: The sack of Lindisfarne (Holy Island) on 8 June 793 is iconic, often marking the symbolic start of the Viking Age in Britain due to its shock value and detailed contemporary accounts.
attribution: By Chris Combe from York, UK - Holy Island Sunrise (again), CC BY 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=42080816

title: Raid on Jarrow - St Paul’s Monastery, Jarrow, Tyne and Wear 794
content: Follow-up raids hit Northumbrian monasteries, including Jarrow (associated with Bede) in 794.
attribution: By Mark Smiles, CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=4528346

title: First Viking winter camp - Isle of Thanet, Kent 850
content: Vikings overwintered on Thanet (then an island) around this time, a shift from hit-and-run raids to longer stays.
attribution: By S. R. Gardiner (d. 1902) - A School Atlas Of English History, Longmans, Green, 1892, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=4174443

title: Great Heathen Army lands - Isle of Thanet (Kent) before crossing to East Anglia 865
content: The Great Heathen Army (a large Danish-led force) landed on Thanet in late 865, extracted tribute, then moved into East Anglia for horses and further campaigns.
attribution: By Hel-hama - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=20042870
alt_text: Battles of the Great Heathen Army

title: Vikings capture York - Eboracum (Roman York), North Yorkshire 866
content: York (Jorvik) fell on 1 November 866 to the Great Army.
attribution: By Yorkshirian at English Wikipedia - File:Kingdom of Jorvik.png, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=61453191

title: Battle of York - Within the city walls of York 867
content: Northumbrian forces attempted to retake York but were defeated inside or at the walls.
attribution: ChrisO, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: King Edmund killed - Thetford, Norfolk (traditional site) 869
content: King Edmund of East Anglia was killed/martyred by Vikings; Thetford is the commonly cited traditional location, though exact details vary in sources.
attribution: By Matthew Paris - https://cudl.lib.cam.ac.uk/view/MS-EE-00003-00059/15, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=135510122
alt_text: Death of Edmund by Matthew Paris

title: Battle of Ashdown - Likely Lowbury Hill or Kingstanding Hill, Berkshire Downs 871
content: Alfred (then prince) and his brother Æthelred defeated Vikings; the precise site is uncertain but often placed in the Berkshire Downs area (e.g., Lowbury Hill or nearby).
attribution: By Richard Doyle (died 1883) - Hughes, T (1859). Scouring the White Horse; Or, the Long Vacation Ramble of a London Clerk., Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=65467313
alt_text: 19th Century Depiction of the Battle of Ashdown

title: Vikings camp for winter - Repton Abbey, Derbyshire (site of a mass Viking grave) 873
content: The Great Army wintered at Repton; archaeological evidence includes a mass grave of Viking warriors.
attribution: By James Yardley, CC BY-SA 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=13793544

title: Viking settlement begins - Primarily the Tyne valley and Yorkshire 876
content: Permanent settlements expanded in northern/eastern England (e.g., Northumbria/Yorkshire) around this period, leading to the Danelaw.
attribution: By Taras Young - Own work, CC BY-SA 4.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=117596025
alt_text: A stone marker shows the source of the River North Tyne

title: Battle of Edington - Bratton Camp (hillfort) or Edington village, Wiltshire 878
content: Alfred's decisive victory over Guthrum; site is debated but often linked to Edington or nearby Bratton Camp hillfort.
attribution: By Marilyn Peddle from England - King Alfred's Tower, CC BY 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=95989707

title: Treaty of Wedmore - Aller (baptism site) and Wedmore, Somerset 878
content: After Edington, Guthrum was baptized at Aller (near Athelney), with confirmation/rituals at Wedmore; this led to the Treaty (or "Peace") of Wedmore, establishing boundaries (Danelaw).
attribution: By Nicolas Eynaud - Own work, CC BY-SA 3.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=25576919

title: Alfred captures London - The City of London (Roman Lundeneburh) 886
content: Alfred retook London from Viking control, marking a key recovery.
attribution: [[File:Engraving of King Alfred the Great by George Vertue for Rapin's History of England.png|Engraving of King Alfred the Great by George Vertue for Tindal's translation of Rapin's History of England.

title: Battle of Brunanburh - Often identified as Bromborough, Wirral 937
content: Athelstan's victory over a coalition (Scots, Strathclyde, Vikings); the location is heavily debated, but Bromborough (Wirral) is a leading scholarly candidate due to linguistic and strategic fit, though alternatives (e.g., elsewhere in northern England) exist.
attribution: Alfred Pearseː The great battle of Brunanburh in 937 (Hutchinsons Story of the British Nation, 1922).jpg

title: Death of Eric Bloodaxe - Stainmore Pass, on the border of Cumbria and Durham 954
content: Eric (last Viking king of York/Jorvik) was killed at Stainmore (or nearby).
attribution: By Clarendon Press (photographer not named) - https://archive.org/details/handbookofcoinso00brituoft, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=5994943

title: Battle of Maldon - Northey Island and the causeway, Essex 991
content: Famous from the poem The Battle of Maldon; Vikings defeated Byrhtnoth's forces at the causeway to Northey Island.
attribution: Alfred Pearse, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons

title: Sweyn recognized as King - Gainsborough, Lincolnshire (his base of operations) 1013
content: Sweyn Forkbeard conquered much of England; Gainsborough was a key base where he received submissions.
attribution: Anonymus (The Life of King Edward the Confessor), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
alt_text: Sweyn Forkbeard from the Life of Edward the Confessor

title: Cnut becomes King - London (following the Battle of Assandun in Essex) 1016
content: After Assandun (near Ashingdon, Essex), Cnut (Canute) secured the throne and was recognized in London.
attribution: David Kemp / Hilltop near Ashingdon

title: Battle of Stamford Bridge, River Derwent crossing - Stamford Bridge, East Riding of Yorkshire 1066
content: Harald Hardrada's invasion ended here; English forces under Harold Godwinson defeated the Vikings at the Derwent crossing.
attribution: By Matthew Paris - http://cudl.lib.cam.ac.uk/view/MS-EE-00003-00059/1, Public Domain, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=21914431
alt_text: Battle of Stamford by Matthew Pairs
